news_summary: Efforts are advancing to address missing persons and displacement in Syria through collaborative international support, alongside calls for the Security Council to transform preventive diplomacy into effective action.
news_summary_fr: Les efforts se poursuivent pour remédier aux problèmes des personnes disparues et des déplacés en Syrie grâce à un soutien international concerté, parallèlement aux appels lancés au Conseil de sécurité pour qu’il traduise la diplomatie préventive en actions concrètes.
news_summary_es: Se están intensificando los esfuerzos para abordar la cuestión de las personas desaparecidas y los desplazamientos en Siria mediante el apoyo internacional colaborativo, al tiempo que se insta al Consejo de Seguridad a que convierta la diplomacia preventiva en medidas efectivas.

articles.title: Syria ‘not alone’ in locating missing persons, tackling displacement
articles.summary: Syria is not alone in tackling challenges, including the hundreds of thousands who remain missing during past tumultuous decades, the head of a UN institution said on Thursday as António Guterres departs on his first visit to the country in his current capacity as Secretary-General.
articles.content:
In a country having among the world’s highest numbers of missing persons, Karla Quintana, head of the UN Independent Institution on Missing Persons in the Syria, provided a snapshot of ongoing efforts.

Having processed more than 75,000 documents and sharing information with key partners, the institution has also been focusing on missing migrants and children and those disappeared by terrorist group Da’esh following the fall of the Assad regime in late 2024.

“Syria knows they don’t have to do this alone,” she explained, noting that the institution, established by the UN General Assembly in 2023, continues to work towards locating missing persons and supporting their families.

Challenges remain

While the new Government has granted some access to the institution, Ms. Quintana explained that national authorities have yet to allow the establishment of an office in the country.

Still, accessing, analysing and sharing information remains key to efforts to discover the fate and whereabouts of missing persons, she said.

In that vein, collaborative efforts are ongoing, she said, emphasising that the families of the missing had been the ones to ask for the creation of the institution.

“We are working to give answers to families,” she said.

Watch the full press conference here:

Tackling displacement

At the same time, Syria has a historic opportunity to transform one of the world’s largest displacement crises into an inclusive and sustainable recovery, an independent human rights expert said on Thursday following an official visit to the country.

“Internally displaced persons are among the most affected by Syria’s past, and among those who can contribute most significantly to its future,” said Paula Gaviria Betancur, the UN Special Rapporteur* on the human rights of internally displaced persons.

An estimated 14 million people have been displaced since 2011. While nearly two million internally displaced persons and 1.7 million refugees have returned since December, about 5.5 million people remain internally displaced, with reconstruction needs standing at $216 billion and 15.6 million people continuing to require humanitarian assistance.

Tweet URL

Supporting rights

“Supporting their human rights, capacities and participation is an investment into Syria’s social fabric, economic recovery and long-term peace,” she said.

An important measure of success will be whether displaced persons can enjoy their rights and rebuild their lives in safety and dignity, she said.

The Special Rapporteur will be presenting her full report to the Human Rights Council in June 2027.

Read her full statement here.

Earlier this month, UN investigators from the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on Syria urged the Government to continue to trace missing detainees and ensure accountability after its week-long visit, marked by deadly bomb attacks in Damascus and insecurity in the restive south.

Read the full story here.

* Appointed by the UN Human Rights Council, Special Rapporteurs are independent human rights experts who are not UN staff and do not receive a salary for their voluntary work.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant ongoing efforts by a UN institution to locate missing persons in Syria and support their families, addressing a major humanitarian and human rights crisis. It also discusses the potential for inclusive recovery for millions of displaced persons, which has broad societal impact and offers hope for long-term peace and reconstruction. The story provides substantial context on the scale of displacement, institutional efforts, and future plans, making it a meaningful positive development in human rights.
articles.personality_title: UN steps up efforts to find missing persons and support displaced Syrians
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Syria has faced years of conflict, leaving hundreds of thousands of people missing and millions displaced. The situation has created one of the world’s biggest humanitarian challenges.

**What happened** – A new UN institution, created in 2023, is working to locate missing persons in Syria and help their families. Karla Quintana, the head of this institution, shared that they have processed over 75,000 documents and focus especially on missing migrants, children, and victims of the terrorist group Da’esh. Although the Syrian government has allowed some access, the UN office has not yet been fully established inside the country.

**Impact** – These efforts are important because they bring hope to families who have been waiting for answers about their loved ones. At the same time, Syria faces a huge task to help millions of displaced people rebuild their lives. An independent UN expert highlighted that supporting displaced Syrians' rights and involvement is key to the country’s recovery and peace.

**What's next step** – The UN Special Rapporteur on internally displaced persons will present a full report to the Human Rights Council in June 2027. Meanwhile, the UN continues pushing for better access in Syria and cooperation to find missing persons and support displaced communities.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN is actively working to locate missing Syrians and support displaced people, aiming to bring answers to families and help build a safer future for the country.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU intensifie ses efforts pour retrouver les personnes disparues et soutenir les déplacés syriens
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Syrie a traversé des années de conflit, laissant des centaines de milliers de personnes disparues et des millions déplacées. Cette situation représente l’un des plus grands défis humanitaires au monde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle institution de l’ONU, créée en 2023, travaille à localiser les personnes disparues en Syrie et à aider leurs familles. Karla Quintana, responsable de cette institution, a indiqué qu’ils ont traité plus de 75 000 documents et se concentrent particulièrement sur les migrants disparus, les enfants et les victimes du groupe terroriste Da’esh. Bien que le gouvernement syrien ait accordé un certain accès, le bureau de l’ONU n’est pas encore entièrement établi dans le pays.

**Impact** – Ces efforts sont importants car ils apportent de l’espoir aux familles qui attendent des réponses sur leurs proches. En même temps, la Syrie doit relever un énorme défi pour aider des millions de déplacés à reconstruire leur vie. Un expert indépendant de l’ONU a souligné que soutenir les droits et la participation des déplacés est essentiel pour la reconstruction et la paix du pays.

**Prochaine étape** – La Rapporteuse spéciale de l’ONU sur les personnes déplacées présentera un rapport complet au Conseil des droits de l’homme en juin 2027. Parallèlement, l’ONU continue de demander un meilleur accès en Syrie et une coopération accrue pour retrouver les disparus et soutenir les communautés déplacées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’ONU travaille activement à retrouver les Syriens disparus et à soutenir les déplacés, pour apporter des réponses aux familles et aider à construire un avenir plus sûr pour le pays.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU intensifica esfuerzos para localizar personas desaparecidas y apoyar a desplazados en Siria
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Siria ha sufrido años de conflicto, dejando a cientos de miles de personas desaparecidas y millones desplazadas. Esta situación representa uno de los mayores desafíos humanitarios del mundo.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva institución de la ONU, creada en 2023, trabaja para localizar a las personas desaparecidas en Siria y ayudar a sus familias. Karla Quintana, directora de esta institución, dijo que han procesado más de 75,000 documentos y se enfocan especialmente en migrantes desaparecidos, niños y víctimas del grupo terrorista Da’esh. Aunque el gobierno sirio ha permitido cierto acceso, la oficina de la ONU aún no se ha establecido completamente en el país.

**Impacto** – Estos esfuerzos son importantes porque traen esperanza a las familias que esperan respuestas sobre sus seres queridos. Al mismo tiempo, Siria enfrenta la gran tarea de ayudar a millones de desplazados a reconstruir sus vidas. Un experto independiente de la ONU destacó que apoyar los derechos y la participación de los desplazados es clave para la recuperación y la paz del país.

**Próximo paso** – La Relatora Especial de la ONU sobre personas desplazadas presentará un informe completo al Consejo de Derechos Humanos en junio de 2027. Mientras tanto, la ONU sigue exigiendo mejor acceso en Siria y cooperación para encontrar a las personas desaparecidas y apoyar a las comunidades desplazadas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La ONU trabaja activamente para localizar a los sirios desaparecidos y apoyar a los desplazados, buscando dar respuestas a las familias y ayudar a construir un futuro más seguro para el país.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a mosaic map of Syria composed of scattered puzzle pieces gently coming together, with glowing silhouettes of diverse families and children emerging from the pieces, surrounded by softly intertwined hands symbolizing collaboration and hope, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Security Council LIVE: How to turn preventive diplomacy rhetoric into action
articles.summary: In the Security Council debate on Thursday on how best to settle disputes between States peacefully, ambassadors provided examples of success and challenges ahead under such themes as effective early warning and how to move from rhetorical support for preventive diplomacy to ensuring more consistent use of its Chapter VI powers under the UN Charter.
articles.content:
What’s on the table?

Council members met to delve into how best to prevent conflict.

Here’s what you need to know:

Chapter VI overview: The UN Charter’s Chapter VI, the Pacific settlement of disputes, empowers the Security Council to call on parties in a dispute to resolve it peacefully before it threatens international peace and security. View Chapter VI here.

Debate objectives:

Identify best practices and gaps in UN Charter-based dispute mechanisms

Explore how diplomacy and mediation can build trust and prevent conflict

Review the implementation of resolution 2788 (2025), which requested the Secretary-General to provide recommendations to further strengthen mechanisms for the peaceful settlement of disputes one year following its adoption

Guiding questions: Ambassadors discussed how to make more consistent use of Chapter VI tools, strengthen the Secretary-General’s good offices, better support Member States through mediation and technical advice, learn from past de-escalation successes and leverage regional and subregional organizations for early, locally-owned conflict resolution.

Broader aim: The debate sought practical recommendations to strengthen the Council’s preventive role and support peaceful pathways in potentially destabilising situations.

Watch the morning meeting here and the afternoon session below:
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a Security Council debate focused on enhancing preventive diplomacy to peacefully settle disputes between states, which has broad significance for global peace and security. It highlights concrete efforts to strengthen mechanisms under the UN Charter for conflict prevention, aiming to reduce violence and promote stability worldwide. This represents a meaningful, substantive step toward peaceful conflict resolution with wide-reaching positive impact.
articles.personality_title: UN Security Council discusses stronger ways to prevent conflicts peacefully
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The United Nations Security Council met to talk about how countries can solve disagreements without fighting. They focused on using a part of the UN Charter called Chapter VI, which helps settle disputes peacefully before they turn into bigger problems.

**What happened** – On Thursday, ambassadors shared examples of what has worked and what still needs improvement in stopping conflicts early. They talked about making better use of tools like mediation, early warnings, and support from the UN Secretary-General’s office. They also reviewed progress since a 2023 resolution aimed at improving peaceful dispute methods.

**Impact** – This meeting is important because it looks for real ways to stop conflicts before they start. By learning from past successes and involving local groups, the Security Council hopes to make peacekeeping more effective and prevent violence worldwide.

**What's next step** – The Council will use the ideas from this debate to create practical recommendations. These will help guide how the UN supports countries in solving problems peacefully, especially in risky situations.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN Security Council is working to turn ideas about preventing conflicts into real actions that help countries settle disputes without violence.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Conseil de sécurité de l’ONU discute de moyens renforcés pour prévenir pacifiquement les conflits
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Conseil de sécurité des Nations Unies s’est réuni pour discuter de la manière dont les pays peuvent résoudre leurs désaccords sans recourir à la violence. Ils se sont concentrés sur l’application d’une partie de la Charte de l’ONU appelée Chapitre VI, qui aide à régler pacifiquement les conflits avant qu’ils ne s’aggravent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jeudi, les ambassadeurs ont partagé des exemples de réussites et des points à améliorer dans la prévention des conflits. Ils ont parlé de mieux utiliser des outils comme la médiation, les alertes précoces et le soutien du bureau du Secrétaire général. Ils ont aussi examiné les progrès réalisés depuis une résolution de 2023 visant à renforcer les méthodes pacifiques de règlement des différends.

**Impact** – Cette réunion est importante car elle cherche des solutions concrètes pour empêcher les conflits avant qu’ils ne commencent. En tirant des leçons des succès passés et en impliquant des groupes locaux, le Conseil espère rendre le maintien de la paix plus efficace et éviter la violence dans le monde.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Conseil utilisera les idées de ce débat pour formuler des recommandations pratiques. Celles-ci aideront à guider la manière dont l’ONU soutient les pays dans la résolution pacifique de leurs problèmes, surtout dans les situations risquées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Conseil de sécurité de l’ONU travaille à transformer les idées de prévention des conflits en actions concrètes pour aider les pays à régler leurs différends sans violence.
articles.personality_title_es: El Consejo de Seguridad de la ONU debate formas más fuertes para prevenir conflictos pacíficamente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Consejo de Seguridad de las Naciones Unidas se reunió para hablar sobre cómo los países pueden resolver sus desacuerdos sin pelear. Se enfocaron en usar una parte de la Carta de la ONU llamada Capítulo VI, que ayuda a solucionar conflictos pacíficamente antes de que se vuelvan problemas mayores.

**Qué pasó** – El jueves, los embajadores compartieron ejemplos de lo que ha funcionado y lo que aún debe mejorar para detener conflictos a tiempo. Hablaron sobre hacer mejor uso de herramientas como la mediación, las alertas tempranas y el apoyo de la oficina del Secretario General. También revisaron los avances desde una resolución de 2023 que buscaba mejorar los métodos pacíficos para resolver disputas.

**Impacto** – Esta reunión es importante porque busca maneras reales de detener conflictos antes de que comiencen. Aprendiendo de éxitos pasados e involucrando a grupos locales, el Consejo de Seguridad espera hacer que el mantenimiento de la paz sea más efectivo y evitar violencia en todo el mundo.

**Próximo paso** – El Consejo usará las ideas de este debate para crear recomendaciones prácticas. Estas guiarán cómo la ONU apoya a los países para resolver problemas pacíficamente, especialmente en situaciones riesgosas.

**Frase clave** – El Consejo de Seguridad de la ONU trabaja para convertir las ideas sobre prevención de conflictos en acciones reales que ayuden a los países a resolver disputas sin violencia.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting showing a round table made of interlocking puzzle pieces, each piece featuring subtle abstract symbols of peace and dialogue, surrounded by softly glowing lanterns that represent mediation and trust-building, set against a calm horizon where multiple gentle rivers converge, symbolizing diverse nations coming together peacefully through preventive diplomacy.
